observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer to determine the composition of the unaccounted balance of P29,629,786.28 and provide necessary details to validate its inclusion in the balance of Land account as of December 31, 2020 for the fair presentation in the financial statements as required under Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
observations.managementAction: Efforts were undertaken to determine the composition of the unaccounted balance of P29,629,786.28 but to no avail.
observations.reason: The composition of the unaccounted balance of P29,629,786.28 was still not determined because there are other concerns that were given priority by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
• Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer to undertake
complete reconciliation of their records by determining the details of the
“Unaccounted” items in their respective records;
• Municipal General Services Officer to complete the conduct of physical
inventory; and
• Municipal Accountant to identify the specific PPE items for entries made in the
ledgers without description, and any adjustments, if warranted, should be
made in order that the existence, accuracy and valuation of the PPE could be
ascertained/established.

We also recommended that the Audit Team be provided with the proof of reconciliation
and adjustments made for verification purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services
Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in order to further determine the
composition of the unaccounted balances in the Land account for its fair presentation
in the Financial Statements, as these balances could be the 45 lots duly supported with
OCT/TCT under the name of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal General
Services Officer to continue exerting efforts to cause the immediate titling of the 16
parcels of land in the name of the Municipality to avoid possible third-party claims that
may arise in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• require the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to prepare an updated IIRUP with
details that match the data reflected in the RPCPPE to serve as an appropriate
and reliable basis in the event of disposal of unserviceable properties, and
submit the same to the Audit Team, together with its supporting documents;
• cause the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties in order to
maximize any realizable value from their disposal by sale in accordance with the
existing regulations;
• require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for
the derecognition of the unserviceable properties upon disposal based on
Paragraph 82 of IPSAS 17; and
• ensure that the disposal of property either thru public bidding or negotiated sale is
properly documented and duly authorized.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor require the:
• Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, the Municipal Agriculturist, the
Municipal Tourism Officer, the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources
Officer to conduct visitorial and monitoring of the projects and to continue exerting
effort to collect the receivables from the concerned beneficiaries.
• Municipal Treasurer to:
➢ prepare schedule of beneficiaries of livelihood projects and payment thereof
per program/project basis and account the nature of livelihood collections
totaling P341,361.91; and
➢ adopt efficient collection strategies to enforce collection from the concerned
beneficiaries to ensure the recovery of the amount released to the
beneficiaries.
• Municipal Accountant to:
➢ continue to reconcile the records with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office; and
➢ conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the
existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ensure
that these are collected when these become due and demandable in
compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer to exert extra effort to undertake complete reconciliation of their records by determining the details of the “Unaccounted” items in their respective records; to determine the unaccounted balances; to provide the Audit Team with the proof of reconciliation and adjustments made for verification purposes; and to set up the provision for Allowance for Impairment and revert to Loans Receivable-Others account the amounts that were reclassified to Other Receivables without valid basis; and adopt programs for sustainability of the livelihood programs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer agreed to undertake complete reconciliation by determining the details of the “Unaccounted” items in their respective records. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant committed to identify the specific PPE items for entries made in the ledgers without description.
observations.reason: Reconciliation, although undertaken is still incomplete. The ``Unaccounted'' items of PPE were trimmed down in their records but there are items were not covered during the conduct of physical inventory.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in order to further determine the composition of the unaccounted balances in the Land account for its fair presentation in the Financial Statements, as these balances could be the 45 lots duly supported with OCT/TCT under the name of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal General Services Officer to continue exerting efforts to cause the immediate titling of the 16 parcels of land in the name of the Municipality to avoid possible third-party claims that may arise in the future.
observations.managementAction: The Management, through the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer, agreed to determine the composition of the unaccounted balances in the Land account, to seek assistance from the Municipal Assessor’s Office as the unaccounted balances could be the 45 lots duly supported with OCT/TCT under the name of the Municipality, and to suggest additional funding for the titling of the lots that are not yet titled.
observations.reason: Of the 22 untitled lots, four were identified to be actually titled and two were titled in CY 2021; the two remaining 16 lots are still not titled in the name of the Municipality; there are still unaccounted balances in both the General and Special Education Funds; difficulty in identifying the unaccounted balances was encountered.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: • require the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to provide the necessary and heavy information lacking in the IIRUP for the proper matching of the unserviceable properties carried in the PPE account; • submit to the Audit Team the duly updated IIRUPs together with its supporting documents; • cause the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties to maximize realizable value from disposal by sale in accordance with existing regulations; • require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the derecognition of the unserviceable properties upon disposal; and • ensure that the disposal of property either thru public bidding or negotiated sale is properly documented and duly authorized.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer, in her letter dated May 10, 2022, mentioned that the IIRUPs previously submitted to the Audit Team relative to the unserviceable PPEs for disposal are awaiting inspection. She further commented that they will again wait for the unserviceable PPEs to be inspected by the COA Technical Services and will then cause the immediate disposal thereof.
observations.reason: The lacking information in the previously submitted IIRUPs rendered them inappropriate to be used as basis for adjusting the accounts; the IIRUP submitted in CY 2021 include several unserviceable properties that are neither in the books of accounts nor in the RPCPPE; disposal has not yet commenced; items are awaiting inspection.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: • Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, the Municipal Agriculturist, the Municipal Tourism Officer, the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer to conduct visitorial and monitoring of the projects and to continue exerting effort to collect the receivables from the concerned beneficiaries. • Municipal Treasurer to: ➢ prepare schedule of beneficiaries of livelihood projects and payment thereof per program/project basis and account the nature of livelihood collections totaling P341,361.91; and ➢ adopt efficient collection strategies to enforce collection from the concerned beneficiaries to ensure the recovery of the amount released to the beneficiaries. • Municipal Accountant to: ➢ continue to reconcile the records with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office; and ➢ conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ensure that these are collected when these become due and demandable in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant, in her letter dated May 11, 2022, informed the Audit Team that the she will continue to reconcile her records with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, and that efficient collection strategies will be adopted by the Management. The Municipal Treasurer, in her reply per letter received by the Audit Team on May 13, 2022, mentioned that they already have an updated schedule of beneficiaries of various livelihood projects and that they are regularly sending notices of delinquency.
observations.reason: Difficulty in enforcing collections from the beneficiaries. Some beneficiaries considered the projects as assistance not loan; reconciliation is still ongoing; some data and beneficiary details are not yet identified.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to continue exerting efforts in completing the reconciliation of the cash accounts and adjust any discrepancies that will be noted in order to establish the correct balances for the fair presentation of the cash accounts in the financial statements in accordance with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
observations.managementAction: Efforts were given by both the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant in completing the reconciliation of the cash accounts. The Municipal Treasurer reconciling the cash accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to record the purchases of supplies and materials initially under the appropriate asset account and recognize the expense account once the inventories are consumed/distributed in accordance with IPSAS 12; and direct the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from procuring goods on reimbursement basis and adhere to the procurement procedures provided in GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 in order to prevent abuses and corruption in the procurement process.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant now records the purchases of supplies and materials initially under the appropriate asset account and recognizes the expense once the inventories are consumed/distributed. The Municipal Treasurer has refrained from reimbursement basis procurement and is adhering to procurement procedures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: • require the Municipal Accountant to set up a receivable account for the market fees of all the market stallholders; • require the Municipal Treasurer to continue to exert effort to collect from delinquent stallholders; and • adopt efficient collection strategies to enforce collection from the concerned stallholders.
observations.managementAction: A receivable account for the market fees was already set up by the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Treasurer is trying to adopt collection strategies and is regularly sending notices to delinquent stallholders. The Municipality adopted efficient collection strategies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to give notice to the payees or holders of checks at least one (1) month before the checks become stale and adjust the affected accounts for stale checks upon return/surrender of the same for cancellation and replacement pursuant to existing regulations on stale checks.
observations.managementAction: The affected accounts have been adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor seek the help/assistance of the Provincial Legal Officer, or the Provincial Prosecutor, or the representation of a private counsel, or any other appropriate action necessary for the repossession of the Municipal Asphalt Plant for the benefits of Mauban and its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor agreed to continue to protect the interest of the Municipality by seeking the help/assistance of the Provincial Legal Officer which resulted as to a scheduled meeting with the owner of PROASPA. However, it was due to the many cases handled by the Provincial Legal Officer that the meeting did not push through.
observations.reason: No proof of communication with the Provincial Legal Officer was provided to the Audit Team; the meeting did not push through due to schedule conflicts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to continue exerting efforts in facilitating the application for insurance coverage of all the insurable properties of the Municipality with the GSIS, including those that are newly acquired, considering that funds have been provided in the CY 2021 budget, in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-390 and RA No. 656.
observations.managementAction: Through the efforts of the Municipal General Services Officer, the Municipality was able to insure various properties in CY 2021.

observations.managementAction: Utilization of LDRRM Fund through proper planning has been maximized in CY 2021.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to monitor the progress of titling of the four lots the documents of which are already in the BIR; and to continue exerting extra effort to cause the immediate titling of the remaining 14 lots in the name of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Monitoring by the General Services Officer to continue. One lot in the amount of P100,000.00 was titled under the name of the Municipality. Partially Implemented
observations.reason: The titling process was affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, hence the same is taking some time to be completed. With the new developments, the audit observation is updated and presented anew under audit observation no. 2, Part II of this Report.

observations.managementAction: The Appraisal and Disposal Committee to accomplish the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties; The Appraisal and Disposal Committee was able to accomplish the required IIRUP. Disposal requested by the Municipality. Partially Implemented
observations.reason: The first IIRUP for disposal was forwarded to the Regional Office No. IV-A and was then referred to the Technical Services, but due to the restrictions brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic, inspection and appraisal could not be done yet. Furthermore, two more letter requests dated November 26, 2020 and December 17, 2020 with attached IIRUPs for disposal were received by the Audit Team on November 27, 2020 and December 21, 2020, respectively. However, upon initial evaluation by the Audit Team, it was noted that information such as Unit Cost, Total Cost, Property Number, Date Acquired, Number of Years and Accumulated Depreciation were lacking for some properties listed in the IIRUPs. This hindered the Audit Team from taking further action such as requesting for the inspection and appraisal of the properties contained in the IIRUPs. Also, the lacking information, if not provided by the Municipality, will also render the IIRUPs inappropriate to be used as basis for adjusting the accounts/dropping from the books of accounts the unserviceable properties carried in the PPE accounts since proper matching could not be made.

         ➢ Municipal Social  Welfare  Development  Officer  to  revise  the

         ➢ Municipal Accountant and the Municipal  Treasurer  to  reconcile

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  require  the  Municipal
   Accountant and the Sangguniang Bayan or ordinance to submit to the  Audit
   Team the approved Sangguniang Bayan resolution relative to the  insurance
   coverage with private insurance company, if there is any, and  the  other
   necessary supporting documents at the soonest  possible  time,  otherwise
   the transaction may be disallowed in audit for lack of authorization.

      Also, we recommended the Municipality to refrain from giving insurance
   coverage without approved Sangguniang Bayan resolution or ordinance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  require  the  Municipal
      Accountant to submit to the Audit Team  all  the  required  supporting
      documents for evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
 • require the Municipal Planning  and  Development  Coordinator  (MPDC)  to
   ensure that the  mandatory  Development  Fund  appropriated  out  of  the
   Internal Revenue Allotment be fully  utilized  to  implement  prioritized
   identified  programs,  projects  and  activities  to  help  achieve   the
   desirable  outcome  on  socio-economic  development   and   environmental
   Management outlined in the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1;

 • install regular monitoring mechanism to assess the  progress  of  project
   implementation so  that  all  its  programmed  development  projects  are
   implemented as programmed to ensure that intended beneficiaries avail  of
   the maximum benefits that will be derived therefrom; and

 • ensure that  a  closer  coordination  among  the  officials  involved  in
   planning, execution and monitoring is enhanced  to  achieve  the  desired
   level of efficiency.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
      Municipal  Treasurer  to  exert  effort  to  collect  from  delinquent
      stallholders the amount of P757,333.00.

            We  also  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  request  the
      Sangguniang Bayan to effect the revision of the Local Revenue Code  to
      attain realistic rates of taxes and enhance collection.

            We further recommended that  the  Municipal  Mayor  require  the
      Municipal Treasurer and the Market Supervisor to:

      ➢ serve the lease contracts as soon as  the  revision  of  the  Local
        Revenue Code was effected in order to protect the interest  of  the
        Municipality against illegal users of municipal amenities and


      ➢ adopt efficient collection strategies to  enforce  collection  from
        the concerned stallholders.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal  Mayor  take the
      appropriate  action/s  under  the  circumstances  to  protect  the
      interest of  Municipality  and  to  avoid  incurrence  of  wastage  of
      resources  which  may  include  following  up  the  appeal  filed  and
      inquiring from the Provincial Fiscal other possible legal remedies .

We  reiterated  our  recommendation  that  the  Municipal  Mayor
      instruct  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to  secure/keep   the   documents
      supporting the Gross Receipts for business tax purposes and update the
      Abstract of Collections from individual taxpayers.

We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  Municipal
      Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO)  and  the  Municipal
      Solid Waste Management Board to secure the  approval  of  its  10-year
      Solid Waste Management Plan from the National Solid  Waste  Management
      Commission pursuant to the provisions of the above-cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  require  the  Municipal
      Engineer to:

 • submit the list of all  ongoing  government  projects/programs/activities
   and those that are to be implemented during the year;

 • submit the monthly monitoring report in compliance with the  above  cited
   regulations; and

 • inform the Auditors that the appropriate project signboards and/or public
   notices were already posted within the prescribed period for validation.
